sample_values: A 2025 report assessing corridor-level speed impacts of different bus lane types, How Much Faster Are We Moving, found that some bus lanes produced speed gains of up to 33% while others saw only modest improvements, or even speed reductions.
A team inbox (opendata@mtahq.org) has worked well for us.
A truck is any vehicle designed to transport property that has more than two axles or six or more tires.

A weighted average of the two Z-scores was then computed for each bus line: 33% weight given to speed, and 66% weight given to reliability. This average z-score was then normalized and rescaled to a 4.0 academic GPA scale. Reliability accounts for two-thirds of a bus line's final grade.
All data visualizations on MTA's performance metrics dashboard (metrics.mta.info) are built using datasets available on the Open Data Portal, and the website is built with open-source tools.
Get in touch with us by emailing opendata@mtahq.org or by filling out this form.

MTA bus schedules, fare collection machines, fare enforcement, or general MTA issues (e.g., MetroCard, OMNY, subway service) please contact the MTA online or call 511

New York City Transit and MTA Bus operate all subways and buses in New York City. Our 45,000 employees serve 4,500,000 passengers a day. We operate nearly 6,700 subway cars and 5,800 buses, and we maintain 472 subway stations, 640 miles of track, 27 bus depots and 70 shops and yards.

The Metropolitan Transportation Authority is North America's largest transportation network, serving a population of 15.3 million people across a 5,000-square-mile travel area surrounding New York City through Long Island, southeastern New York State, and Connecticut.

sample_values: 34th Street Transitway project presented to Manhattan Community Board 5 as the Locally Preferred Alternative selected by the 34th Street Alternatives Analysis.

A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

A five-year transportation plan to improve the safety, accessibility, and quality of the City's streets for all New Yorkers. The plan involves an in-depth analysis of the current state of New York City's streets and responds equitably to the uniqueness of the City's many neighborhoods and local needs.

Busway pilot along Jay Street from Smith St./Livingston St. to Jay St./Tillary St. Part of Better Buses Restart. NYC is speeding up implementation on bus projects citywide to provide faster and more reliable bus service for essential workers and communities impacted by COVID-19.
Comprehensive performance review of the NYC bus system published in April 2024 (sic) by the NYC Comptroller's Office. The Life in the Slow Lane report card builds on this analysis.

Selected BRT alternative for 34th Street following alternatives analysis screening. Two-way Transitway aligned along curb for length of 34th Street. Transitway occupies half of street, north half east of Fifth Avenue, south half east of Sixth Avenue, with a new plaza from Fifth to Sixth. General Traffic: One-Way Eastbound only east of Fifth Avenue, One-Way Westbound only west of Sixth Avenue.

Tremont Ave. will become a busway eastbound from Third Ave. to Southern Blvd. and westbound from Southern Blvd. to Belmont Ave., designed to increase bus speeds and reliability for 34,000+ daily bus riders on the Bx36 and improve truck movement and deliveries to local businesses.
18-month pilot project making 14th Street between 9th Avenue and 3rd Avenue a Transit & Truck Priority corridor starting October 3, 2019.
accepted_sample_values: 18-month pilot project making 14th Street between 9th Avenue and 3rd Avenue a Transit & Truck Priority corridor starting October 3, 2019.
2018 NYC Department of Transportation plan that established a performance target of increasing bus speeds by 25% citywide—from approximately 8 to 10 miles per hour. Target has since been abandoned.
2021 NYC DOT initiative to install nine new major bus lanes and busways throughout the city. Extended and made permanent the 14th Street Busway and created plans to implement five additional busways.
2025 report assessing corridor-level speed impacts of different bus lane types in NYC.
34th Street Transitway project presented to Manhattan Community Board 5 as the Locally Preferred Alternative selected by the 34th Street Alternatives Analysis.

A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.
A bus-only street in Lower Manhattan, from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m., only trucks and buses are allowed to travel through the corridor. Started as a pilot in 2019, later made permanent.

A five-year transportation plan to improve the safety, accessibility, and quality of the City's streets for all New Yorkers. The plan involves an in-depth analysis of the current state of New York City's streets and responds equitably to the uniqueness of the City's many neighborhoods and local needs.
canonical_sample_values: 2021 NYC DOT initiative to install nine new major bus lanes and busways throughout the city. Extended and made permanent the 14th Street Busway and created plans to implement five additional busways.
2025 report assessing corridor-level speed impacts of different bus lane types in NYC.

A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.
A new pedestrian plaza in Washington Heights, completed in 2025.
A new section of the Harlem River Greenway on Bailey Avenue in the Bronx.
A protected bike lane on Court Street in Brooklyn.
A report card assessing every bus line in NYC on speed, bunching rate, and on-time performance using real-time MTA data from June 2024 to June 2025, assigning letter grades A through F.
A transformational Bike Boulevard on 31st Avenue in Astoria, completed in 2025.
